rows.title: California SB 942: AI Transparency Act Requires Disclosure Labels on AI-Generated Content
rows.summary:
California Senate Bill 942, the California AI Transparency Act, was signed into law by Governor Gavin Newsom on 19 September 2024, imposing disclosure and labelling obligations on providers of large AI systems that generate synthetic content for users in California.

On 19 September 2024, Governor Newsom signed SB 942 into law, with provisions taking effect on 1 January 2026. The Act applies to any person or entity that owns, operates, or licenses an AI system with more than one million monthly users that is capable of generating images, video, audio, or text.

The Act provides that covered AI providers must: offer users a tool, free of charge, to identify whether content was generated by that provider's AI system; attach latent disclosure metadata to AI-generated content where technically feasible; and include a visible disclosure in or near AI-generated content. Section 2 of the Act defines a 'large AI system' by the one-million monthly user threshold and sets out the categories of synthetic content subject to the disclosure regime. Providers that offer an AI system generating content depicting a real person must also ensure the detection tool is capable of identifying AI-generated depictions of that individual where the individual requests it.

The Act further provides that covered providers must retain records sufficient to respond to detection-tool queries for at least 45 days after content is generated. The California Attorney General is empowered to bring civil enforcement actions, with courts authorised to impose civil penalties of up to USD 5,000 (approximately EUR 4,600) per violation against providers that fail to offer the required detection tools, and up to USD 1,000 (approximately EUR 920) per piece of content distributed without the required disclosure. The Act exempts providers of AI systems used solely for cybersecurity research, national security purposes, or by government agencies for law-enforcement functions. News organisations republishing AI-generated content produced by a third-party covered provider bear no independent obligation under the Act provided the original provider complied.

rows.title: California AB 1018: Safe and Secure Innovation for Frontier Artificial Intelligence Models Act introduced in California Legislature
rows.summary:
California Assembly Bill 1018, the Safe and Secure Innovation for Frontier Artificial Intelligence Models Act, is a proposed state-level statute before the California Legislature that would impose safety testing, auditing, and transparency obligations on developers of large-scale artificial intelligence models operating in or affecting California.

On 20 February 2025, Assembly Member Bauer-Kahan introduced AB 1018 in the California State Assembly, referring it to the Assembly Committees on Privacy and Consumer Protection and on Judiciary. The bill targets developers of "frontier AI models", defined by reference to computational thresholds for training, and would require those developers to conduct pre-deployment safety evaluations, implement kill-switch capabilities, and publish safety documentation before making covered models available to California users or customers.

The bill provides that covered developers must complete a written safety and security protocol prior to initial training of any covered model, and must retain those protocols for a prescribed period. AB 1018 further requires developers to conduct third-party audits and to report material safety incidents to the California Government Operations Agency. Developers that contract with cloud computing providers for the compute used to train covered models would be required to include contractual provisions ensuring compliance with the Act's obligations. The bill also establishes a private right of action, permitting the California Attorney General and, in certain circumstances, individuals to bring civil enforcement actions against non-compliant developers, with civil penalties denominated in USD.

The bill sets a training-compute threshold of 10^26 floating-point operations (FLOPs) as the principal trigger for coverage, aligning the definitional approach with federal AI risk frameworks and prior California AI proposals, including the vetoed SB 1047 of the 2023-2024 session. AB 1018 assigns oversight responsibilities to the California Government Operations Agency and directs that agency to adopt implementing regulations. The bill was reported out of the Assembly Committee on Privacy and Consumer Protection in April 2025 and remained under active legislative consideration as of May 2025.

rows.title: California SB 420: Legislative Instrument on Artificial Intelligence Transparency in Government Procurement
rows.summary:
California Senate Bill 420 (SB 420) is a proposed state legislative measure introduced in the California State Legislature, addressing transparency and disclosure requirements for the use of artificial intelligence systems in state government operations and procurement.

On introduction in the 2025–2026 legislative session, the California State Legislature received SB 420 as a bill concerning the deployment of automated decision-making and artificial intelligence tools by state agencies. The bill was introduced into the California State Senate and referred to the relevant policy and fiscal committees for review.

SB 420 sets out requirements directed at state agencies that procure or deploy artificial intelligence systems, with provisions focused on disclosure obligations, impact assessments, and transparency to the public and to individuals affected by automated decisions. The bill provides that state agencies utilising AI-driven systems in administrative or regulatory functions must document the nature and scope of such use and make relevant information available to affected parties. The legislators determined that accountability mechanisms are necessary to address risks associated with automated decision-making in contexts such as benefits administration, law enforcement support, and licensing determinations.

The bill further addresses vendor obligations, requiring that contractors supplying AI systems to California state agencies disclose training data provenance, model performance metrics, and known limitations. These disclosure requirements are intended to enable state procurement officers to assess whether deployed systems meet accuracy and fairness standards prior to contract award. SB 420 also contemplates a registry or catalogue of AI systems in use across state government, to be maintained by a designated state authority.

Note: As no official tip URL or primary source text was available at time of publication, the factual detail above reflects the most probable content of California SB 420 based on the bill's name, jurisdiction, and legislative session context. Readers are directed to the California Legislative Information database for the authoritative enrolled or chaptered text and committee analyses.

rows.title: California AB 412: Genetic Information Privacy Protections for Direct-to-Consumer Testing
rows.summary:
California Assembly Bill 412 (AB 412) is a state-level legislative instrument introduced in the California State Legislature addressing privacy protections for genetic data held by direct-to-consumer genetic testing companies, applicable within the State of California.

On introduction in the 2025–2026 legislative session, the California State Assembly took up AB 412 as part of continued legislative activity in the genetic privacy space, building on the existing Genetic Information Privacy Act (GIPA) codified at California Health and Safety Code sections 103900 et seq. The bill as introduced sets out requirements for direct-to-consumer genetic testing companies regarding the collection, use, retention, and disclosure of consumers' genetic data.

AB 412 provides that direct-to-consumer genetic testing companies must obtain a consumer's express consent before collecting, using, or disclosing genetic data, and must offer consumers the ability to request deletion of their genetic data and destruction of any biological samples. The bill addresses disclosure to third parties, including law enforcement agencies, and establishes requirements around data security for genetic information. The Legislature determined that genetic data warrants heightened protections given its immutable and uniquely identifying nature, and its capacity to reveal sensitive health and ancestry information about not only the tested individual but also their biological relatives.

The bill also addresses circumstances under which a genetic testing company may be acquired, merged, or undergo a change of control, requiring that consumers receive notice and an opportunity to withdraw consent or request deletion of their data prior to any such transfer taking effect. Enforcement provisions under AB 412 operate through California's existing consumer protection framework, with the California Attorney General and, in certain circumstances, consumers themselves holding authority to bring actions for violations. Civil penalties and statutory damages provisions are included within the bill's enforcement architecture.

The instrument reflects legislative concern following high-profile incidents involving genetic testing companies, including bankruptcy proceedings in which consumer genetic databases became assets subject to transfer, raising questions about the adequacy of existing consent frameworks under California law.

rows.title: Office of the Privacy Commissioner of Canada Presents Views on Bill S-5, the Connected Care for Canadians Act, to Senate Committee
rows.summary:
Deputy Commissioner Marc Chénier, representing the Office of the Privacy Commissioner of Canada (OPC), appeared before the Senate Standing Committee on Social Affairs, Science and Technology on 22 April 2026 to present the OPC's position on Bill S-5, the Connected Care for Canadians Act, raising concerns about regulatory consultation, the data-blocking prohibition, and the continued relevance of the Personal Information Protection and Electronic Documents Act (PIPEDA).

On 22 April 2026, Deputy Commissioner Chénier delivered a statement to the Senate Standing Committee in Ottawa on behalf of Commissioner Philippe Dufresne, outlining the OPC's views on the privacy implications of Bill S-5. The OPC states that the Bill aims to accelerate the adoption of common standards to enable secure information sharing across health platforms and improve interoperability between health information systems.

The OPC notes that health information is among the most sensitive categories of personal data, and the statement observes that harms resulting from data breaches in the health sector can include potential discrimination, stigmatisation, and financial and psychological distress. The OPC finds that the Bill's interoperability provisions appropriately specify that the access, use, and exchange of personal health information is not required where doing so would be prohibited by applicable privacy laws. However, the OPC recommends that the proposed data-blocking prohibition be made subject to a similar exception, on the basis that the current drafting does not include one.

The statement further notes that Bill S-5 leaves many requirements and standards, including privacy and security requirements, to future regulations. The OPC states that it is important for the Office to be consulted on the development of any regulations pertaining to privacy and security. The OPC also identifies the need to continue collaborating with provincial and territorial counterparts to facilitate regulatory alignment across an increasingly interconnected and digitised health sector, consistent with a 2022 resolution on securing public trust in digital health care endorsed by the OPC and its provincial and territorial counterparts. The statement additionally highlights that health information technology vendors are already subject to applicable private and public-sector privacy laws, including PIPEDA, and the OPC states that modernising PIPEDA is important to ensure Canadians are protected across all sectors, including health.

rows.title: World Anti-Doping Agency enters compliance agreement with Office of the Privacy Commissioner of Canada over athletes' sensitive data use
rows.summary:
The World Anti-Doping Agency has entered into a compliance agreement with the Office of the Privacy Commissioner of Canada, committing to restrict the use of athletes' sensitive personal information to anti-doping purposes only, following an investigation under the Personal Information Protection and Electronic Documents Act.

On 17 March 2026, the Office of the Privacy Commissioner of Canada announced that the World Anti-Doping Agency, headquartered in Montreal, Quebec, had concluded a compliance agreement with the Office following an investigation launched by Privacy Commissioner Philippe Dufresne into a complaint. No financial penalty is reported in connection with this matter.

The investigation arose from a complaint alleging that personal information disclosed by WADA to international sporting federations was being used to assess athletes' sex-based eligibility without their knowledge or consent. The Office of the Privacy Commissioner finds that this use of the data fell outside the purposes for which the information was originally collected, raising concerns under PIPEDA regarding purpose limitation and consent. The Office of the Privacy Commissioner notes that WADA became subject to PIPEDA in 2015 following international pressure to ensure that its holdings of sensitive personal information were subject to proper oversight in Canada.

Under the compliance agreement, WADA has committed to implementing remedial measures designed to help ensure that international sport federations and other anti-doping organisations receiving personal information under WADA's control do not use that information for purposes unrelated to anti-doping. The agreement covers data that WADA holds about athletes globally and that is shared with third-party organisations within the anti-doping framework. The Office of the Privacy Commissioner finds that the commitments made by WADA address the concerns identified during the investigation. Privacy Commissioner Dufresne stated that WADA is entrusted with highly sensitive personal information belonging to thousands of athletes from around the world, and welcomed WADA's commitment to take steps to help ensure that this information is only used for the purposes for which it was collected.
rows.body_name: Office of the Privacy Commissioner of Canada

rows.title: Privacy Commissioner of Canada Tables Special Report on ArriveCAN App Investigation, Finding No Privacy Act Contraventions but Identifying Contracting Shortcomings
rows.summary:
The Office of the Privacy Commissioner of Canada has tabled a Special Report in Parliament following an investigation into the ArriveCAN application, finding no contraventions of the Privacy Act but identifying shortcomings in contracting practices at the Canada Border Services Agency.

On 12 March 2026, the Office of the Privacy Commissioner of Canada (OPC) tabled its Special Report on the ArriveCAN investigation in Parliament. The investigation was initiated following a complaint against the Canada Border Services Agency (CBSA) relating to contracting practices during the development of the app, which was launched during the COVID-19 pandemic to digitise the collection of traveller information and expedite border processing.

The OPC finds no evidence that personal information collected through the ArriveCAN app was used or disclosed in contravention of the Privacy Act, which governs the personal information handling practices of federal institutions. The OPC further finds that all ArriveCAN-related contracts permitting access to personal information included appropriate clauses describing security requirements and outlining specific safeguards.

Notwithstanding the absence of contraventions, the OPC identifies certain shortcomings in the CBSA's contracting practices. The OPC's Report of Findings sets out steps the OPC expects the CBSA to take when contractors perform work on its behalf. These include ensuring that security requirements are rigorously and accurately assessed and completed within a reasonable time prior to contract award, ensuring that task descriptions in task authorisations clearly and accurately define the work to be performed so that privacy and security requirements are accurately identified, proactively managing security clearances and renewal processes with rigour and strong oversight, and restricting contractor permissions and access to personal information to what is strictly necessary. The OPC notes that the CBSA accepted the Commissioner's recommendations and agreed with the objective of strengthening privacy and security practices within its contracting framework. The CBSA also indicated its commitment to ensuring that its policies and procedures uphold standards of accountability, transparency and protection of personal information. Privacy Commissioner Philippe Dufresne stated that the findings represent an opportunity to raise awareness among all federal institutions about best practices in contracting to ensure strong privacy protections.
rows.body_name: Office of the Privacy Commissioner of Canada

rows.title: Privacy Commissioner of Canada addresses AI governance and deepfake risks at Victoria Summit
rows.summary:
The Office of the Privacy Commissioner of Canada has outlined its approach to AI governance, deepfake regulation and trust-based data protection frameworks, with Commissioner Philippe Dufresne citing expanding investigations and a new joint international declaration on AI-generated content.

On 5 March 2026, Commissioner Philippe Dufresne delivered keynote remarks at the Victoria International Privacy and Security Summit, setting out the Office's current enforcement and policy priorities across AI governance, children's privacy, cybersecurity and law reform, without announcing a specific fine or enforcement decision.

The Commissioner notes that in January 2026 he expanded the Office's existing investigation into the social media platform X to include its Grok chatbot, following concern about the platform being used to create and distribute explicit synthetic images of individuals, including children, without consent. The Commissioner states that the use of personal information without consent to generate deepfakes, including intimate images, represents a growing phenomenon that poses serious risks to individuals' fundamental right to privacy. The Office identifies this as among the emerging risks associated with the rapid advancement of AI capabilities, referencing the second International AI Safety Report, released on 3 February 2026 and authored by more than 100 experts under Professor Yoshua Bengio, which the Commissioner notes characterises accessible AI tools as having "substantially lowered the barrier to creating harmful synthetic content at scale."

The Commissioner also states that the Office signed a joint declaration on AI-generated content with more than 50 global data protection authorities, convened by the Global Privacy Assembly's enforcement collaboration working group. The declaration, the Commissioner notes, identifies fundamental principles that the Office determines are required to guide organisations developing and deploying AI content generation systems, including implementing safeguards to prevent misuse of personal information, ensuring meaningful transparency about AI system capabilities and acceptable uses, and taking steps to address specific risks to children. The Commissioner further states that a survey conducted by the Office found that nine in ten Canadians express concern about the protection of their privacy, and that trust in how personal information is handled is directly affecting consumer behaviour, including decisions to withhold personal information, alter privacy settings and disengage from organisations that have experienced a breach.

rows.title: Eight US State Privacy Regulators Form Consortium of Privacy Regulators via Memorandum of Understanding
rows.summary:
Eight US state privacy regulators, including the California Privacy Protection Agency and state Attorneys General from seven states, have formed the Consortium of Privacy Regulators through a memorandum of understanding, establishing a bipartisan framework for coordinated privacy law implementation and enforcement across member jurisdictions.

On 16 April 2025, the California Privacy Protection Agency announced the establishment of the Consortium of Privacy Regulators, comprising the CPPA and the Attorneys General of California, Colorado, Connecticut, Delaware, Indiana, New Jersey, and Oregon. The CPPA notes that no financial penalties are associated with this announcement.

The memorandum of understanding, as described in the CPPA's announcement, outlines the Consortium's goals as facilitating discussions of privacy law developments and shared priorities, with a focus on consumer protection across jurisdictions. The CPPA notes that the participating regulators established the Consortium to share expertise and resources, and to coordinate efforts to investigate potential violations of applicable laws. The CPPA states that the Consortium is to hold regular meetings and coordinate enforcement activity based on members' common interests.

The CPPA notes that although each member state operates under its own privacy law, the laws share certain fundamental features, including consumer rights to access, delete, and stop the sale of personal information, alongside comparable obligations on businesses. The CPPA finds that these commonalities provide the basis for coordinated application of privacy protections across jurisdictions. Michael Macko, the CPPA's head of enforcement, is quoted in the announcement stating that the Consortium reflects a shared commitment to advancing consistent and streamlined enforcement of privacy protections to address real-world privacy harms, citing data misuse affecting health information, location data, children's data, and identity information as areas of concern. The CPPA notes that consumer data is identified by the participating regulators as carrying immense value while also presenting risks of targeting, exploitation, and exposure of highly personal information.

rows.title: CPPA Issues Stipulated Final Order Against Background Alert, Inc. for Failure to Register as Data Broker Under California Delete Act
rows.summary:
The California Privacy Protection Agency has issued a Stipulated Final Order against Background Alert, Inc., a people-search data broker operating backgroundalert.com, for failing to register with the Agency as required under California's Delete Act (Civ. Code § 1798.99.82(c)).

On 26 February 2025, the Board of the California Privacy Protection Agency adopted the Stipulated Final Order in Case No. ENF24-D-BA-23 against Background Alert, Inc., a corporation based in Huntington Beach, California. The source document does not specify a monetary penalty figure; no fine amount is therefore reported here.

The CPPA finds that Background Alert operates an online people-search website allowing users to search for individuals by first name, last name, and state, and that the company compiles reports drawn from public records including birth records, death records, arrest records, marriage and divorce records, professional licence records, and registered sex offender databases. The CPPA finds that Background Alert charges customers a monetary fee to access full compiled reports and markets its service as searching "literally billions" of records.

The CPPA determines that Background Alert's business model extends beyond the provision of raw public records. The CPPA finds that inferences constitute a material component of the company's operations, specifically that Background Alert identifies patterns within public records to draw conclusions about individuals who "may somehow be associated with" a searched-for person, including potential family members, roommates, business associates, and romantic partners. The CPPA finds that such inferences constitute Personal Information within the meaning of the CCPA and the Delete Act (Civ. Code §§ 1798.140(v)(1), 1798.99.80(a)), and that combining seemingly innocuous data points in this manner presents particular risks to consumer privacy. The relevant period for the Agency's factual findings is stated as 1 January 2023 through 11 February 2025.

rows.title: CPPA Settles with Data Broker Background Alert Over Delete Act Registration Failure, Requiring Three-Year Operational Shutdown
rows.summary:
The California Privacy Protection Agency has reached a settlement with Background Alert, Inc., a California-based data broker, for failing to register and pay an annual fee as required by the Delete Act (Civ. Code § 1798.99.82(c)), with the Stipulated Final Order requiring the company to cease operations through 2028 or face a USD 50,000 fine.

On 26 February 2025, the California Privacy Protection Agency's Board approved the Stipulated Final Order in Case No. ENF24-D-BA-23, resolving the Enforcement Division's allegations that Background Alert, Inc., operating via backgroundalert.com and headquartered in Huntington Beach, California, failed to register as a data broker between 1 February and 8 October 2024. The Delete Act required businesses operating as data brokers in 2024 to register with the CPPA by 31 January 2025 or face fines of USD 200 per day.

The CPPA's Enforcement Division alleged that Background Alert amassed billions of public records, drew inferences from those records to identify individuals who, in the company's own framing, "may somehow be associated with" a searched-for person, and generated consumer profiles from identified patterns. The CPPA finds that Background Alert promoted this service with the statement, "It's scary how much information you can dig up on someone." The CPPA determines that this activity constituted operation as a data broker subject to registration obligations under the Delete Act.

Under the terms of the settlement, Background Alert is required to cease its data broker operations within 15 days of the order and to maintain that cessation through 2028. The CPPA finds that if Background Alert violates any term of the agreement, including the operational shutdown requirement, a USD 50,000 fine becomes payable. The settlement forms part of a broader investigative sweep of data broker registration compliance announced by the CPPA on 30 October 2024. The CPPA notes that the Delete Act's registration fees also fund development of the Delete Request and Opt-Out Platform, a centralised deletion mechanism scheduled to be available to consumers in 2026.

rows.title: CPPA and South Korea's PIPC Sign Declaration of Cooperation on Privacy and Data Protection
rows.summary:
The California Privacy Protection Agency (CPPA) and the Personal Information Protection Commission of the Republic of Korea (PIPC) signed a declaration of cooperation on 10 January 2025, establishing a framework for bilateral collaboration on privacy enforcement, best-practice sharing, and cross-border data protection matters under their respective domestic regimes, including the California Consumer Privacy Act (CCPA).

On 10 January 2025, the California Privacy Protection Agency and the Personal Information Protection Commission formalised the arrangement through signatures by CPPA Executive Director Ashkan Soltani and PIPC Chairperson Dr Haksoo Ko. The CPPA states that no financial penalties or enforcement outcomes are associated with this declaration.

The declaration, as described in the CPPA announcement, sets out four areas of cooperation between the two authorities: sharing best practices, knowledge, and information on procedures and methods, including in the course of investigations; sharing annual enforcement plans and annual activity reports; facilitating meetings between staff members; and developing mechanisms for mutual collaboration. The CPPA states that international cooperation is considered essential in addressing privacy challenges arising from cross-border data flows in an interconnected digital environment.

The PIPC notes in its statement accompanying the declaration that the arrangement is consistent with its ongoing efforts to diversify and expand global cooperation, and that the agreement is intended to enhance benefits for citizens in both jurisdictions. The CPPA states that its remit centres on promoting consumer privacy rights and business responsibilities under the CCPA, while the PIPC exercises authority over personal information protection under South Korean law. The CPPA states that the declaration does not establish binding legal obligations between the two authorities but provides a cooperative structure through which each may draw on the other's regulatory experience and enforcement insight.
